APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper falls squarely within top-tier NLP/ML venues: it tackles semantic parsing to SQL with neural sequence-to-sequence models, compares to established baselines (e.g., Dong & Lapata, Jia & Liang, Zettlemoyer & Collins), and evaluates on standard datasets (GEO880, ATIS) plus a novel live user deployment. The experimental setup and evaluation metrics (execution accuracy, user-judged correctness, annotation rates) meet community standards, hyperparameters are reported, and related work is thoroughly covered. From an empirical-methodology perspective I note some reproducibility concerns (code release not mentioned) and noise in user feedback are acknowledged, but these do not undermine the paper’s clear relevance and fit for a top NLP/ML conference. | B(5): This paper addresses a core NLP/ML problem—semantic parsing to SQL—with a practical, deployment-oriented interactive learning loop that is highly relevant to top-tier venues. It evaluates with standard benchmarks (GEO880, ATIS), execution accuracy metrics, and a real-world multi-stage online experiment, and it tackles practitioner-facing concerns (user feedback noise, annotation budget, schema template generation). The focus on direct SQL generation, closed-loop feedback, and measured user interaction positions it squarely within the interests of the conference audience and research community.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper falls squarely within top-tier NLP/ML venues: it tackles semantic parsing to SQL with neural sequence-to-sequence models, compares to established baselines (e.g., Dong & Lapata, Jia & Liang, Zettlemoyer & Collins), and evaluates on standard datasets (GEO880, ATIS) plus a novel live user deployment. The experimental setup and evaluation metrics (execution accuracy, user-judged correctness, annotation rates) meet community standards, hyperparameters are reported, and related work is thoroughly covered. From an empirical-methodology perspective I note some reproducibility concerns (code release not mentioned) and noise in user feedback are acknowledged, but these do not undermine the paper’s clear relevance and fit for a top NLP/ML conference.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper tackles an important, practical problem—bootstrapping deployable semantic parsers for new SQL domains with minimal intervention—demonstrated both on benchmarks (GEO880, ATIS) and a live multi-stage deployment reaching 63.5% correctness. The closed-loop use of noisy binary user feedback plus selective crowdsourced annotation is a compelling, reproducible workflow that is likely to change practitioner approaches to rapid-domain adaptation and encourage follow-up work on feedback-driven training. While not clearly transformative (limitations around feedback noise, execution failures, and template scalability are acknowledged), the approach is broadly usable and opens concrete research directions in interactive dataset curation and robust feedback aggregation. | B(4): This paper tackles an important, practical problem—rapidly deploying semantic parsers for new database domains—with a realistic, feedback-driven pipeline that combines direct neural SQL generation, user binary feedback, and selective crowd annotation. The live three-stage experiment and improvements from iterative feedback (reaching ~63.5% correct) demonstrate practical feasibility and lower annotation burden, which should influence practitioners building production NL-to-SQL systems. While not overturning core modeling paradigms, the work bridges research and deployment and opens productive directions for human-in-the-loop learning and schema-aware data augmentation; limitations (noisy feedback, schema-template issues on ATIS) temper claims but do not negate the broader applicability.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper tackles an important, practical problem—bootstrapping deployable semantic parsers for new SQL domains with minimal intervention—demonstrated both on benchmarks (GEO880, ATIS) and a live multi-stage deployment reaching 63.5% correctness. The closed-loop use of noisy binary user feedback plus selective crowdsourced annotation is a compelling, reproducible workflow that is likely to change practitioner approaches to rapid-domain adaptation and encourage follow-up work on feedback-driven training. While not clearly transformative (limitations around feedback noise, execution failures, and template scalability are acknowledged), the approach is broadly usable and opens concrete research directions in interactive dataset curation and robust feedback aggregation.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles an important, practical problem—rapidly deploying semantic parsers for new database domains—with a realistic, feedback-driven pipeline that combines direct neural SQL generation, user binary feedback, and selective crowd annotation. The live three-stage experiment and improvements from iterative feedback (reaching ~63.5% correct) demonstrate practical feasibility and lower annotation burden, which should influence practitioners building production NL-to-SQL systems. While not overturning core modeling paradigms, the work bridges research and deployment and opens productive directions for human-in-the-loop learning and schema-aware data augmentation; limitations (noisy feedback, schema-template issues on ATIS) temper claims but do not negate the broader applicability.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by experiments across simulated benchmarks (GEO880, ATIS) and a live three-stage deployment, demonstrating iterative gains (63.51% correct after three stages) and comparison to relevant baselines. Mathematical content is minimal and appears consistent; the core contribution is empirical. However, the experimental methodology has some gaps for full reproducibility and rigor: code is not reported released, there is no mention of statistical significance testing, and user-feedback noise and execution errors (22.3% non-executing queries, noisy labels) introduce potential confounds that are acknowledged but not fully controlled. Overall the results justify the conclusions qualitatively, but the lack of released code, formal significance analysis, and remaining dataset/feedback biases prevent a technically flawless evaluation. | B(4): The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and largely supported by experiments on GEO880/ATIS and a live user deployment demonstrating iterative improvement to 63.51% accuracy, and the methodological choices (direct SQL generation, feedback loop, selective crowdsourcing) are evaluated with appropriate baselines. There are no formal mathematical derivations to verify, and empirical methodology is generally sound (reasonable baselines and execution-based evaluation), but some technical gaps remain: user feedback is noisy and a substantial fraction of generated queries failed to execute (22.3%), schema-template benefits did not generalize to ATIS, and the paper does not report statistical significance testing or fully explore the impact of noisy feedback on learning dynamics. Overall the conclusions are supported but tempered by these practical limitations, so I judge the work technically strong with minor issues.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by experiments across simulated benchmarks (GEO880, ATIS) and a live three-stage deployment, demonstrating iterative gains (63.51% correct after three stages) and comparison to relevant baselines. Mathematical content is minimal and appears consistent; the core contribution is empirical. However, the experimental methodology has some gaps for full reproducibility and rigor: code is not reported released, there is no mention of statistical significance testing, and user-feedback noise and execution errors (22.3% non-executing queries, noisy labels) introduce potential confounds that are acknowledged but not fully controlled. Overall the results justify the conclusions qualitatively, but the lack of released code, formal significance analysis, and remaining dataset/feedback biases prevent a technically flawless evaluation.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and largely supported by experiments on GEO880/ATIS and a live user deployment demonstrating iterative improvement to 63.51% accuracy, and the methodological choices (direct SQL generation, feedback loop, selective crowdsourcing) are evaluated with appropriate baselines. There are no formal mathematical derivations to verify, and empirical methodology is generally sound (reasonable baselines and execution-based evaluation), but some technical gaps remain: user feedback is noisy and a substantial fraction of generated queries failed to execute (22.3%), schema-template benefits did not generalize to ATIS, and the paper does not report statistical significance testing or fully explore the impact of noisy feedback on learning dynamics. Overall the conclusions are supported but tempered by these practical limitations, so I judge the work technically strong with minor issues.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper introduces a notable, practically useful idea—an interactive, feedback-driven training loop that combines binary user feedback with selective crowd annotation to quickly adapt neural seq2seq models for new SQL domains; this operational closed-loop deployment and live multi-stage experiment is not standard in the cited prior semantic-parsing work. Directly generating full SQL with neural models has antecedents, but the combination with schema-template synthetic data and the deployment/feedback pedagogy constitutes a non-trivial extension beyond prior papers (e.g., Dong & Lapata, Jia & Liang) rather than a purely incremental reapplication. An expert would be informed by the live deployment results and the pragmatic pipeline design, even if core modeling elements reuse existing seq2seq/attention techniques. | B(4): The paper combines known components (seq2seq neural parsers mapping to logical forms) with a practical, deployment-focused closed loop of binary user feedback plus selective crowdsourced correction and direct SQL generation—an application-oriented synthesis that is notably different from prior academic setups. Directly generating SQL from anonymized utterances and using lightweight user feedback to drive iterative improvement addresses a practical gap and is a non-trivial, clearly differentiated extension beyond existing semantic parsing work (e.g., Dong & Lapata; Poon). While each element is rooted in established techniques, their integration and live three-stage deployment evaluation provide meaningful novelty that would inform practitioners and researchers.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is generally well organized and presents a clear logical flow from problem motivation to method and experiments (including simulated benchmarks and a live three-stage deployment), with its main contributions stated early. Notation and experimental details (e.g., hyperparameters reported, datasets ATIS/GEO880, execution-accuracy metric) are clear, and figures/tables/examples appear informative; related work coverage is thorough. A few minor presentation issues remain — for example, the handling of noisy user feedback and failure modes could be made even clearer (some quantified noise rates are given), and the absence of an explicit code release is noted in the summary — but overall the writing is good and unambiguous. | B(4): The paper is generally well organized and easy to follow: contributions are stated early, the method-to-experiment narrative is logical, and experiments (including live deployment) are clearly described. Notation and hyperparameters are reported consistently, and figures/tables/examples appear informative; related work is thorough. Minor issues remain (e.g., some limits of schema templates on ATIS and noisy user-feedback details could be spelled out more precisely), but overall the writing quality is good and clarity is high.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper provides a solid experimental evaluation: simulated benchmarks (GEO880, ATIS), comparisons to multiple prior systems (e.g., Poon 2013, Dong & Lapata 2016, Jia & Liang 2016), and a live three-stage online deployment with measured user feedback (including quantified noise rates and 22.3% non‑executing queries). It includes useful sensitivity observations (e.g., limits of schema templates and their diminishing benefit) and reports hyperparameters, and it discusses failure modes arising from incomplete DB content and noisy user labels. Missing, however, is explicit mention of code/data release and some reproduction-critical details (training/test split specifics and the full annotation/selection protocol) that would strengthen reproducibility; overall the experiments are thorough but there are minor gaps in reproducibility and ablation depth. | B(4): The paper presents a solid experimental evaluation: benchmark results on GEO880/ATIS plus an ecologically valid three-stage online deployment with quantified user-feedback noise and execution failure rates, supporting the main claims about interactive learning. It includes relevant sensitivity analysis (e.g., impact of schema templates and how their benefit changes with labeled data) and reports hyperparameters, while related work appears thoroughly covered. Limitations and failure modes are explicitly discussed (user mislabeling, non-executing queries, template shortcomings), but some practical reproduction details are missing (code availability not mentioned, limited detail on crowdsourcing protocol and error-handling), and the ATIS template failure suggests a need for deeper analysis of multi-entity queries. Overall the empirical work is strong with minor gaps in reproducibility and deeper ablations on error sources.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper provides a solid experimental evaluation: simulated benchmarks (GEO880, ATIS), comparisons to multiple prior systems (e.g., Poon 2013, Dong & Lapata 2016, Jia & Liang 2016), and a live three-stage online deployment with measured user feedback (including quantified noise rates and 22.3% non‑executing queries). It includes useful sensitivity observations (e.g., limits of schema templates and their diminishing benefit) and reports hyperparameters, and it discusses failure modes arising from incomplete DB content and noisy user labels. Missing, however, is explicit mention of code/data release and some reproduction-critical details (training/test split specifics and the full annotation/selection protocol) that would strengthen reproducibility; overall the experiments are thorough but there are minor gaps in reproducibility and ablation depth.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a solid experimental evaluation: benchmark results on GEO880/ATIS plus an ecologically valid three-stage online deployment with quantified user-feedback noise and execution failure rates, supporting the main claims about interactive learning. It includes relevant sensitivity analysis (e.g., impact of schema templates and how their benefit changes with labeled data) and reports hyperparameters, while related work appears thoroughly covered. Limitations and failure modes are explicitly discussed (user mislabeling, non-executing queries, template shortcomings), but some practical reproduction details are missing (code availability not mentioned, limited detail on crowdsourcing protocol and error-handling), and the ATIS template failure suggests a need for deeper analysis of multi-entity queries. Overall the empirical work is strong with minor gaps in reproducibility and deeper ablations on error sources.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against a range of appropriate baselines including classical parsers (Zettlemoyer & Collins, GUSP/GUSP++) and more recent neural approaches (Dong & Lapata 2016; Jia & Liang 2016) on standard datasets (GEO880, ATIS) using execution accuracy, so the baseline set and metrics are largely appropriate and comparable. The authors also analyze when their schema-template augmentation helps (and when it does not, e.g., ATIS) and acknowledge noisy user feedback and failure modes, indicating awareness of prior methods’ strengths and limitations. I rate it one notch down from perfect because the summary does not explicitly state whether all experimental conditions (training data, anonymization, or any domain-specific preprocessing) were standardized across comparisons, so minor fairness or omitted-baseline concerns may remain. | B(5): The paper benchmarks against a strong set of prior systems (GUSP, GUSP++, Dong & Lapata 2016, Jia & Liang 2016, Zettlemoyer & Collins 2007) on standard datasets (GEO880, ATIS) using execution accuracy, so comparisons are appropriate and in-line with community practice. The authors fairly evaluate under the same data and metrics, explicitly discuss cases where prior methods are competitive or where their own design (schema templates, direct SQL generation) has limitations (e.g., ATIS multi-entity queries), and provide a thorough related-work discussion—indicating comprehensive and fair comparisons with relevant baselines.

The authors also analyze when their schema-template augmentation helps (and when it does not, e.g., ATIS) and acknowledge noisy user feedback and failure modes, indicating awareness of prior methods’ strengths and limitations. I rate it one notch down from perfect because the summary does not explicitly state whether all experimental conditions (training data, anonymization, or any domain-specific preprocessing) were standardized across comparisons, so minor fairness or omitted-baseline concerns may remain.

The authors fairly evaluate under the same data and metrics, explicitly discuss cases where prior methods are competitive or where their own design (schema templates, direct SQL generation) has limitations (e.g., ATIS multi-entity queries), and provide a thorough related-work discussion—indicating comprehensive and fair comparisons with relevant baselines.

main_claims: A feedback-based interactive learning algorithm enables rapid deployment of semantic parsers for new database domains with minimal intervention.
Neural sequence-to-sequence models can be adapted to directly generate full SQL queries from natural language, avoiding intermediate meaning representations.

User feedback plus selective crowdsourced annotation forms a closed loop that improves parser accuracy over successive deployment stages; demonstrated both in simulated benchmarks (GEO880, ATIS) and a live three-stage online experiment (reaching 63.51% correct after three stages).
_summary.methodology: Train a neural sequence-to-sequence encoder-decoder model with attention to map anonymized natural language utterances directly to SQL using synthetic data from schema templates and paraphrases, deploy it to users, collect binary correct/incorrect feedback, add correct (utterance, predicted SQL) pairs to training data and send incorrect ones for crowd annotation, then retrain iteratively.
metrics: Execution accuracy (execute predicted SQL and compare results)
limitations: User feedback is noisy: 6.1% of correct queries were marked wrong and 6.3% of incorrect queries were marked correct, and 22.3% of generated queries did not execute.
Schema templates did not improve accuracy on ATIS (limitation for multi-entity queries) and their benefit diminishes as labeled data grows.
Some errors arise from incomplete database content for aggregation queries, causing users to mislabel results.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - semantic parsing (natural language to SQL)

reviewer_a.name: Empirical Methodologist
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with a decade of experience building and evaluating production NLP systems and running large-scale experiments; has published and reviewed extensively on reproducibility and experimental design in ML/NLP venues.
traits: expects clear descriptions of training/test splits, hyperparameters, and code/data release for reproducibility
looks for strong baselines, ablation studies, and statistical significance when claiming improvements
evaluates evaluation protocols (e.g., simulated vs. live experiments) and potential biases in feedback/labeling pipelines
checks whether reported gains could arise from dataset artifacts, model capacity, or engineering choices rather than the proposed method
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on the rigor of experiments, clarity of methodology, and whether claims are supported by appropriate comparisons and analyses.

reviewer_b.name: Applied NLP Strategist
reviewer_b.background: Senior researcher in semantic parsing and human-in-the-loop systems with a track record of work on natural language interfaces to databases and user-facing deployments.
traits: assesses novelty relative to prior semantic parsing and interactive learning work and how this paper advances the field
values practical impact: deployment feasibility, user burden, annotation cost, and real-world evaluation
notices how design choices (e.g., direct SQL generation, anonymization, feedback signal) align with or diverge from existing paradigms
considers limitations and generalizability across domains, and whether the approach opens new research directions
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Evaluates significance and fit within the research landscape, balancing novelty and practical contribution while highlighting broader implications and limitations.
